title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The High-Tech Arena: Innovation in Athletics

texts.A: The introduction of sophisticated performance-tracking analytics has undoubtedly transformed elite sports, but we must ask at what cost to the 'spirit' of competition. [37]While some maintain that data-driven coaching ensures a fairer playing field, I would argue that it reduces athletes to mere biological machines, stifling the intuitive flair that defines true greatness.[/37] Furthermore, the financial barrier to accessing such tech is widening the gap between wealthy nations and the rest of the world. [38]Unlike those who see technology as a secondary factor in victory, I believe we are approaching a point where the quality of a team's software might be more decisive than the talent of its players.[/38] We are witnessing the dawn of an era where championships are won in the lab, not on the pitch. This technological arms race is not leveling the playing field but tilting it irreversibly toward the highest bidder. Sports governing bodies must act decisively to introduce spending caps on analytical technology, ensuring that competition remains a test of human endeavor rather than financial dominance.
texts.B: The panic over 'technological doping' is largely misplaced. Throughout history, sport has always evolved alongside human ingenuity, from the first spiked running shoes to carbon-fiber tennis rackets. [39]The real issue is not the technology itself, but the lack of universal standards for its implementation.[/39] [40]Rather than dehumanizing the athlete, wearable sensors and real-time biometric feedback allow for a more personalized approach to health, significantly reducing the career-ending injuries that plagued previous generations.[/40] [37]I share the view that these advancements promote a more meritocratic environment by minimizing the role of luck and maximizing the rewards for scientific preparation.[/37] When athletes can monitor their own physiological responses in real time, they are better equipped to push their limits safely and recover more efficiently than ever before. The real winners of this revolution are not just the teams at the top, but every athlete who can now train smarter and live a longer, healthier sporting life. Progress is inevitable, and trying to halt it is a futile exercise in nostalgia.
texts.C: There is a growing obsession with the quantification of athletic performance that threatens to alienate the fan base. When every movement is measured and every tactic is dictated by an algorithm, the unpredictability that makes sport compelling begins to evaporate. [38]However, I disagree with the notion that technology has become the primary driver of success; at the end of the day, a computer cannot simulate the psychological grit required to perform under pressure.[/38] [39]My concern lies in the uneven regulatory landscape; as long as different leagues have different rules about which gadgets are permitted, the integrity of global records remains questionable.[/39] We need a global governing body to harmonize the use of tech before the results of our competitions become meaningless. Without a unified international framework, the legitimacy of world records will always be tainted by suspicion. Sport's most precious commodity is not speed or strength, but trust—and once spectators begin to doubt whether they are watching athletes or algorithms, the emotional bond that makes sport meaningful will be irrevocably broken.
texts.D: Technology is often blamed for 'ruining' the purity of sport, but this ignores the immense benefits it brings to spectator engagement and officiating accuracy. Video assistant referees and ball-tracking systems have virtually eliminated the human errors that used to spark decades of resentment. [40]Moreover, the data gathered is not just for coaches; it allows fans to appreciate the incredible physical feats of athletes in ways that were previously impossible.[/40] However, we must be careful not to let the pursuit of perfection overshadow the human element. [37]I am less convinced than others that technology levels the playing field; if anything, it provides a massive, unfair advantage to those few organizations with the capital to invest in the latest equipment.[/37] The soul of sport is its accessibility, and that is what is currently at risk. We must therefore insist on transparency in how analytical tools are developed and deployed. If the same innovations that protect a wealthy club's star striker are made available to every league worldwide, technology could yet fulfil its democratic promise.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on whether technology makes sports competition fairer?
questions.explanation: B believes technology promotes a 'meritocratic environment' (fairer). D, however, argues that it provides an 'unfair advantage' to those with capital, thus making it less fair.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to A regarding the relative importance of technology and athlete talent in winning?
questions.explanation: A believes technology is becoming 'more decisive than the talent of its players.' C disagrees, stating that 'a computer cannot simulate the psychological grit' which remains the primary driver of success.

questions.question: Which expert shares C's concern about the need for standardized rules for sports technology?
questions.explanation: C calls for 'harmonizing the use of tech' due to 'uneven regulatory landscapes.' B similarly states that the real issue is 'the lack of universal standards for its implementation.'

questions.question: Which expert has a similar view to B on how technology affects the well-being or appreciation of the individual athlete?
questions.explanation: B emphasizes that technology helps in 'reducing career-ending injuries' (well-being). D mentions that it allows fans to 'appreciate the incredible physical feats' of the athletes. Both see a positive impact on the perception or condition of the individual athlete.
